title-group.article-title: Probiotic bacteria Bifidobacterium bifidum upregulation of intestinal epithelial tight junction barrier is mediated by TLR-2/TLR-6 receptor complex activation of occludin gene

abstract.p: Defective intestinal epithelial tight junction (TJ) barrier is a key pathogenic factor of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). Probiotic bacterial upregulation of intestinal TJ barrier has been shown to prevent the development of intestinal inflammation. However, the mechanism of microbe-host interactions responsible for the TJ barrier upregulation remains unclear. This study investigates the molecular mechanisms by which a particular strain of probiotic bacteria, Bifidobacterium bifidum (BB1), upregulates the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier. Using in vitro (filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers) and in vivo (recycling intestinal perfusion in live mice) intestinal epithelial model system, we show that BB1 upregulation of intestinal TJ barrier correlated with an increase in occludin gene activity (occludin promoter activity and occludin mRNA transcription levels) and protein expression, with no changes in other TJ proteins. Occludin knockdown or inhibition of gene transcription prevented the enhancement of the TJ barrier, confirming the essential role of BB1-induced occludin gene activation in TJ barrier enhancement, which was mediated sequentially by BB1 activation of the intestinal epithelial cell TLR-2/TLR-6 complex and IRAK-1 phosphorylation, as well as the apical membrane recruitment of the adapter protein TOLLIP. These findings provide novel mechanistic insight into the microbe-host interactions driving probiotic bacteria upregulation of intestinal TJ barrier.

institution-wrap.institution: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: A defective intestinal epithelial TJ barrier is a key pathogenic factor of intestinal inflammation in various intestinal diseases, including inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), and celiac disease1–3. Tight junctions are the apical-most junctional complexes and serve as a structural and functional barrier regulating the paracellular permeation of luminal substances through the intercellular spaces of adjacent intestinal epithelial cells4–6. Patients with IBD or NEC have a defective intestinal TJ barrier or “leaky gut” which allows paracellular permeation of noxious luminal substances, including bacterial antigens and digestive enzymes, that leads to immune activation and inflammatory response3,7–11. Previous studies have shown that the IBD patients who are in clinical remission but continue to have elevated intestinal permeability have a poor clinical outcome and an early recurrence of the disease12–14. In contrast, patients who have normalization of their intestinal TJ barrier following medical treatment have an excellent outcome and prolonged clinical remission2,15. Similarly, in various mouse models of IBD, therapeutic tightening of the intestinal TJ barrier prevents the development of intestinal inflammation16,17. Despite the well-established importance of the defective intestinal TJ barrier in the pathogenesis of IBD, there are no approved medical therapies that specifically target the TJ barrier.

Gut microbiome plays a crucial role in maintaining gut homeostasis and barrier function, modulating immune responses and tolerance, and influencing various physiological processes and disease states18. Probiotics are live bacteria that confer health benefits to the host when consumed19,20. Important benefits of probiotics to gut health include the improvement of the intestinal barrier, promotion of healthy gut microflora, and anti-inflammatory and immune-regulating properties21–23. Studies in animal models of intestinal inflammation have demonstrated therapeutic benefits in various intestinal diseases that are associated with increased intestinal permeability or leaky gut, including IBD, NEC, and antibiotic-associated colitis21,24–27. Because probiotics are generally considered safe to consume and many reside in the human gut, they are ideally suited as a naturally occurring biological product for therapeutic purposes19. The beneficial effects and biological activities of probiotics are strain-specific and do not extend broadly across the species28. In recognition of the strain-specificity of probiotic biological activity and health benefits, the European Food Safety Authority and the FDA currently require mechanistic and outcome-based studies that directly demonstrate the strain-specific and cause-and-effect relationship regarding the purported biological activity or therapeutic efficacy to support the health benefit claims of the probiotic products19,20,29,30. Thus, for therapeutic usage, strain-specific mechanism of action, health benefits, and clinical outcomes need to be demonstrated.

Recent studies have shown that a particular Bifidobacterium bifidum strain designated as BB1 produces a unique strain-specific marked enhancement of intestinal epithelial TJ barrier and protects against the development of dextran sodium sulfate (DSS)-induced colitis by preserving the intestinal barrier25,26. In contrast, a Bifidobacterium bifidum strain designated as BB4, which does not affect the TJ barrier, did not protect against the DSS-induced colitis25,26. The mechanisms by which the probiotic bacteria, including BB1, produce the enhancement of the intestinal TJ barrier remain unclear. The major purpose of this study was to determine the molecular mechanisms and the bacterial-host interaction responsible for the BB1 upregulation of intestinal epithelial tight junction barrier, using both cell culture and animal model of intestinal epithelial barrier. The results of this study indicate that BB1-mediated upregulation of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier depends on activation of the occludin gene via the intestinal epithelial cell TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex. Binding of BB1 to the host TLR-2/TLR-6 receptor complex leads to activation and recruitment of the anti-inflammatory adaptor protein TOLLIP and the downstream activation of the occludin gene.

sec.title: BB1 induced enhancement of the Caco-2 intestinal epithelial TJ barrier is mediated by an increase in occludin gene activity
sec.p:
In previous studies, we reported that BB1 (1 × 10⁸ CFU/mL), but not BB4, treatment for 24 hours produced an increase in Caco-2 TJ barrier function, as evidenced by a 70–100% increase in transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and a decrease in transepithelial flux of the paracellular marker inulin25. In the following studies, the effects of BB1 or BB4 on intestinal epithelial TJ protein expression, including transmembrane proteins (claudin-1, claudin-2, claudin-4, claudin-5, claudin-8, and occludin), cytoplasmic protein zonula occludens-1 (ZO-1), and TJ effector protein myosin light chain kinase (MLCK) were determined in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers. Neither BB1 nor BB4 treatment significantly affected the expression of claudin-1, claudin-2, claudin-4, claudin-5, claudin-8, ZO-1, or MLCK, as shown by immunoblot analysis (Fig. 1A) and densitometry (data not shown). Interestingly, BB1, but not BB4, produced an increase in occludin expression (Fig. 1A,B). The effect of BB1 on junctional localization of occludin was also examined in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers by immunostaining with occludin antibody. BB1, but not BB4, enhanced occludin localization and increased the intensity of occludin staining at the apical junctions. (Fig. 1C, D). Next, the requirement for an increase in occludin on BB1 upregulation of Caco-2 TJ barrier function was determined by siRNA-induced knockdown of occludin in Caco-2 monolayers. The occludin siRNA transfection produced a near-complete depletion of occludin in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers by 48 h (Fig. 1E), and this occludin knockdown prevented the BB1 increase in Caco-2 TEER and decrease in trans-epithelial inulin flux (Fig. 1F, G), confirming that the increase in occludin was required for the BB1 upregulation of the Caco-2 TJ barrier. Next, to delineate the mechanism of BB1 increase in occludin protein, the effect of BB1 on occludin gene activity was determined by measuring occludin promoter activity and mRNA expression in the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers. For the occludin promoter activity studies, a 2023-bp occludin promoter region (GenBank accession no. DQ264390) was cloned into pGL-3 basic vector and transfected into filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers as previously described31. The effect of BB1 on occludin promoter activity was assessed by determining the luciferase (reporter gene) activity31. BB1 produced a rapid increase in occludin promoter activity as evidenced by the increase in luciferase activity (Fig. 1H). Correlating with the increase in occludin promoter activity, BB1 also produced an increase in occludin mRNA (Fig. 1I). To confirm the requirement of occludin mRNA transcription for BB1 upregulation of the Caco-2 TJ barrier, Caco-2 monolayers were pretreated with actinomycin-D (a potent gene transcription inhibitor). The actinomycin-D (10 ng/ml) pre-treatment inhibited the BB1-induced increase in occludin mRNA and Caco-2 TEER, confirming the requirement of gene transcription for the BB1 increase in occludin and Caco-2 TJ barrier (Fig.1J,K).Fig. 1Effect of Bifidobacterium bifidum strains BB1 and BB4 on expression of tight junction (TJ) proteins in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers.
BB1 caused an increase in occludin protein expression but did not affect claudin-1, -2, -4, -5, and -8, ZO-1, or MLCK protein expression in Caco-2 monolayers as assessed by Western blot analysis. BB4 had no effect on TJ protein expression (A). Densitometry analysis of BB1 effect on Caco-2 occludin protein levels (B). Effect of BB1 or BB4 on occludin junctional localization in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers as determined by immunostaining (C). Control (Con), BB1, or BB4 treated cells were stained for occludin (green), actin (red), and nuclei (blue). Data are representative of three to five independent experiments. (Leica SP8 confocal microscope) Scale bar = 25 µm. Quantification of the intensity of occludin immunostaining using ImageJ. ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant) (D). The siRNA-induced knockdown of occludin caused a near-complete depletion of occludin (48 h treatment) in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers (E) and inhibited the BB1-induced increase in Caco-2 TEER (F) and decrease in trans-epithelial flux of inulin (G). Effect of BB1 on occludin promoter activity in Caco-2 monolayers (H). The occludin promoter activity was determined by the reporter gene luciferase activity and expressed as relative luciferase activity. The assay was performed using the pGL-3 basic luciferase reporter vector. BB1 caused an increase in occludin mRNA levels, as assessed by real-time PCR. ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control (I). Actinomycin-D inhibited the BB1-induced increase in occludin mRNA expression (J) and Caco-2 TEER (K). ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control.

sec.title: BB1 upregulation of the occludin gene is mediated by TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex
sec.p:
The BB1 upregulation of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier was previously shown to be mediated in part by the attachment of live BB1 cells to the TLR-2 receptor complex on the apical membrane surface of the intestinal epithelial cells25. In the following studies, we investigated the possible regulatory involvement of the TLR-2 receptor complex in the upregulation of the occludin gene. TLR-2 monomers form a biologically active heterodimer receptor complex by binding to either TLR-1 or TLR-632,33. BB1 binding and activation of TLR-2 heterodimeric complexes, TLR-2/TLR-1 or TLR-2/TLR-6, were determined using the bacterial ligand-TLR-2 complex binding and activation assay (InvivoGen, San Diego, CA). For the TLR-2 heterodimer binding and activity studies, transgenic immortalized human embryonic kidney-293 (HEK-293) cell lines expressing either TLR-2/TLR-6 with TLR-1 knockout (TLR-1-/-) or TLR-2/TLR-1 with TLR-6 knockout (TLR-6-/-) on the cell membrane surface linked to the secreted embryonic alkaline phosphatase (SEAP) reporter gene were used34. The SEAP gene is activated by the TLR-2 signal-transduction pathway, activation of NF-κB33. The BB1 addition to the TLR-2/TLR-6 (TLR-1-/-) expressing HEK-293 cells increased SEAP activity, but not in the TLR-2/TLR-1 (TLR-6-/-) expressing HEK-293 cells (Fig. 2A). The addition of Pam3CSK4, a potent TLR-2/TLR-1 agonist used as a positive control, induced the SEAP activation in TLR-2/TLR-1 (TLR-6⁻/⁻) HEK-293 cells (Fig. 2A). These results confirmed that BB1 binds to and activates the TLR-2/TLR-6, but not TLR-2/TLR-1, heterodimer complex. Next, to confirm the requirement of TLR-2/TLR-6 or TLR-2/TLR-1 heterodimer complex in BB1 upregulation of TJ barrier, the effect of selective siRNA KD of TLR-2, TLR-1, or TLR-6 was determined in Caco-2 monolayers. The TLR-2, TLR-1, or TLR-6 siRNA transfection produced a near-complete depletion of the targeted TLR in the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers (data not shown). The siRNA KD of TLR-2 or TLR-6 produced a near-complete inhibition of BB1 upregulation of occludin mRNA, occludin protein, and Caco-2 TEER (Fig. 2B–D). In contrast, TLR-1 KD did not inhibit the BB1 increase in occludin mRNA, protein, or Caco-2 TEER (Fig. 2B–E), suggesting that TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex but not TLR-2/TLR-1 was required for the BB1 upregulation of occludin and Caco-2 TJ barrier.Fig. 2Effect of BB1 on TLR-2 receptor complex activation.
(A) Effect of BB1 on TLR-2/TLR-6 or TLR-2/TLR-1 heterodimer binding and signal-transduction pathway activation as determined by SEAP (secreted embryonic alkaline phosphatase) activity in TLR-1 or TLR-6 knock-out HEK-293 reporter cell lines (InvivoGen, San Diego, CA). BB1 produced a marked increase in SEAP activity in TLR-2/TLR-6 (TLR-1-/-), but not TLR-2/TLR-1 (TLR-6-/-), expressing HEK-293 cells. PAM3cBK4 (positive control) produced an increase in SEAP activity in TLR-2/TLR-1 (TLR-6 -/-) HEK-293 cells. The siRNA knock-down of TLR-1 or TLR-6, but not TLR-2, inhibited the BB1-induced increase in occludin mRNA (B) and protein and densitometry analysis (C, D) and Caco-2 TEER (E) ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant).

sec.title: Upregulation of OCLN gene and Caco-2 TJ barrier by BB1/TLR-2/TLR-6 complex is dependent on adaptor protein TOLLIP but not MyD88
sec.p:
In immune cells, TLR-2 signal transduction pathway activation of the inflammatory response is mediated by MyD88-adaptor protein stimulation of pro-inflammatory genes35. To determine the regulatory requirement of MyD88 in BB1/TLR-2 heterodimer complex upregulation of occludin gene and Caco-2 TJ barrier, the BB1 effect on MyD88 pathway activation was assessed by IRAK-4 phosphorylation in Caco-2 monolayers. IRAK-4 is a downstream substrate of MyD88 and, upon MyD88 activation, undergoes enzymatic phosphorylation at Thr34536,37. In the control Caco-2 monolayers, there was a minimal baseline phosphorylation of IRAK-4 (Thr345) (Fig. 3A). LPS (0.3 ng/mL), a known MyD88 activating ligand used as a positive control, produced a marked increase in IRAK-4 (Thr345) phosphorylation (Fig. 3A). Neither BB1 nor BB4 produced an increase in Caco-2 IRAK-4 phosphorylation (Fig. 3A), indicating the absence of MyD88 activation. Next, the possible regulatory requirement of MyD88 was further assessed by siRNA-induced silencing of MyD88 in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers. The MyD88 siRNA transfection produced a near-complete depletion of MyD88 in Caco-2 monolayers (Fig. 3B). The siRNA-induced MyD88 knockdown did not inhibit the BB1 upregulation of occludin mRNA or Caco-2 TEER (Fig. 3B, C), suggesting that MyD88 is not required for the BB1 upregulation of occludin gene or Caco-2 TJ barrier.Fig. 3Effect of BB1 on Caco-2 MyD88 pathway activation.
A The effects of BB1, BB4, or LPS (a positive control) on IRAK-4 phosphorylation as assessed by Western blot. BB1 or BB4 had no effect on IRAK4 phosphorylation (Thr345) in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers. LPs caused a marked increase in IRAK-4 phosphorylation. MyD88 siRNA transfection resulted in a near-complete depletion of MyD88 in Caco-2 monolayers (B). The siRNA knock-down of MyD88 did not inhibit the BB1 upregulation of occludin mRNA (C) or TEER (D) in the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers. ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant).

Toll-interacting protein (TOLLIP) is an anti-inflammatory adaptor protein recruited by the TLR-2 complex and acts as a negative regulator of MyD88 and inflammation38,39. In the following studies, the possible involvement of TOLLIP in BB1 interaction with the TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex upregulation of occludin gene and Caco-2 TJ barrier was determined. The effect of BB1 or BB4 on TOLLIP activation/ apical membrane recruitment was determined by immunostaining with TOLLIP antibody in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers35. The Caco-2 monolayers were co-labeled with TLR-2 antibody (red) to localize the apical membrane surface (x-z plane, Fig. 4A) and to assess the response to BB1 attachment to the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers (Fig. 4A, B). In the control Caco-2 monolayers, TLR-2 (red) were diffusely present at the apical membrane surface (Fig. 4A). In the untreated control Caco-2 monolayers, TOLLIP (green) were present throughout the cytoplasm from basal to apical locations (x-z plane, Fig. 4A). The BB1, but not BB4, addition to the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers produced a rapid (within 30 minutes) aggregation of TLR-2 at discrete points on the apical membrane surface (corresponding to the points of BB1 attachment) as previously described (Fig. 4A)25; BB1, but not BB4, addition produced a rapid (within 30 minutes) translocation of TOLLIP from the basal and medial regions of the cytoplasm to the apical regions and the apical membrane surface (Fig. 4A). The determination of the relative TOLLIP distribution at the apical regions by quantitation of TOLLIP immunostaining at the apical surface, indicated that BB1 produced a rapid shift of TOLLIP translocation (from 20% to 60%) from the medial and basal regions to the apical region of Caco-2 monolayers (Fig. 4B). The siRNA-induced knockdown of TLR-2 prevented the TOLLIP translocation to the apical region (Fig. 4A, B), indicating that the TOLLIP apical translocation was dependent on the TLR-2 presence at the apical membrane surface. Next, to determine the regulatory role of TOLLIP in BB1-induced upregulation of the occludin gene and the Caco-2 TJ barrier, the effect of TOLLIP knockdown by siRNA on BB1-induced upregulation of occludin and the Caco-2 TJ barrier was assessed. The siRNA KD of TOLLIP inhibited the BB1 increase in occludin mRNA and protein and Caco-2 TEER (Fig. 4C–F), confirming that TOLLIP was required for the BB1 increase in occludin and Caco-2 TJ barrier.Fig. 4Effect of BB on TOLLIP activation/apical membrane translocation in Caco-2 monolayers.
A BB1, but not BB4, treatment produced a focal aggregation of TLR-2 at the Caco-2 apical membrane surface and translocation of TOLLIP towards the apical membrane surface from the medial and basal regions of Caco-2 cells, as assessed by immunostaining. (TOLLIP, green; TLR-2 red; and DAPI blue). Scale bars = 10 μm. Quantification of TOLLIP apical translocation immunostaining intensity using ImageJ. ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant) (B). The siRNA knockdown of TLR-2 or IRAK-1 inhibited the BB1-induced apical translocation of TOLLIP. The siRNA-induced of TOLLIP inhibited the BB1-induced increase in occludin mRNA (C) and protein expression (D), densitometry (E) and Caco-2 TEER (F). ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant).

Adaptor proteins TOLLIP and IRAK-1 co-exist as a bound complex in the cytoplasm of immune cells and have been shown to have an interdependent relationship40,41. In the following studies, the effect of BB1 on IRAK-1 activation was determined by IRAK-1 (Thr209) or IRAK-1 (Thr387) phosphorylation. Thr209 is located on the N-terminal kinase domain of IRAK-1 and has critical role in the kinase activity, whereas Thr387 is in the C-terminal domain and functions in signal propagation and protein-protein interaction42. BB1, but not BB4, produced an increase (within 1 h) in IRAK-1 (Thr209) phosphorylation in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers (Fig. 5A). BB1 or BB4 did not affect IRAK-1 (Thr387) phosphorylation (Fig. 5A). The siRNA knockdown of IRAK-1 inhibited the BB1-induced apical membrane aggregation of TLR-2 and translocation of TOLLIP (Fig. 4A) and prevented the BB1 upregulation of occludin mRNA and protein and Caco-2 TEER (Fig. 5B–E). In separate studies, the siRNA-induced knockdown of TLR-2 or TLR-6, but not TLR-1, inhibited the BB1-induced IRAK-1 phosphorylation (Fig. 5F), confirming the regulatory requirement of TLR-2/TLR-6 complex for the IRAK-1 phosphorylation.Fig. 5Effect of BB1 on IRAK-1 phosphorylation in Caco-2 monolayers.
A BB1, but not BB4, caused an increase in IRAK-1 phosphorylation (Thr209) as assessed by immunoblotting. The siRNA-induced knockdown of IRAK-1 inhibited the BB1-induced increase in occludin mRNA (B), occludin protein (C), densitometry (D) and Caco-2 TEER (E) (means ± SE, n = 4). ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant). The siRNA-induced knockdown of TLR-2 or TLR-6, but not TLR-1, inhibited the BB1 increase in IRAK-1 phosphorylation, but had no effect on total IRAK-1 protein expression (F).

sec.title: BB1 upregulation of mouse intestinal TJ barrier in vivo is regulated by intestinal epithelial cell TLR-2 complex
sec.p:
The above studies demonstrated that the BB1 enhancement of the Caco-2 TJ barrier was dependent on TLR-2/TLR-6 complex and TOLLIP-mediated activation of the occludin gene. In the following studies, the effects of BB1 administration on occludin and the intestinal TJ barrier were examined in vivo in live mice. The effect of BB1 oral-gastric gavage administration (1 × 109 CFU/mL) on the mouse intestinal epithelial TJ barrier was determined in vivo by recycling perfusion of an isolated small intestinal segment (6 cm linear length) in live mice as previously described25,41. The oral-gastric administration of BB1, but not BB4, produced a marked decrease in mouse intestinal permeability as measured by the intestinal permeation of a commonly used paracellular marker, FITC-labeled-dextran 10 kDa (Fig. 6A). BB1, but not BB4, produced an increase in occludin mRNA and protein in the mouse intestinal tissue (Fig. 6B–D). The BB1 effect on intracellular expression and localization of occludin in mouse intestinal epithelial cells was examined by immunostaining with occludin antibody in the mouse intestinal tissue. As shown in Fig. 6E, BB1, not BB4, produced an increase in the intensity and localization of occludin labeling (green) at the apical membrane surface and apical junctional areas of intestinal epithelial cells. Next, the BB1 effect on TOLLIP apical membrane translocation in intestinal epithelial cells was determined. In control mice, TOLLIP (green) were located diffusely in the cytoplasm of intestinal epithelial cells and was mostly absent from the apical membrane surface (actin, stained in red). BB1 administration produced a rapid translocation of TOLLIP (green) from the cytoplasm to the apical membrane of intestinal epithelial cells, as evidenced by TOLLIP co-localization (yellow/orange) with actin (red) at the apical membrane surface (Fig. 6F). BB4 did not produce TOLLIP apical membrane translocation (Fig. 6F).Fig. 6Effect of BB1 on mouse small intestinal permeability and occludin expression.
A Oral -gastric gavage of BB1 [1 × 109 colony-forming units (CFUs)/mL], but not BB4, produced a decrease in mouse small intestinal permeability to dextran 10 kDa. P **** < 0.0001 versus control. BB1 administration caused an increase in occludin mRNA (B) and protein (C), densitometry (D) in the mouse intestinal tissue (24-hour treatment period). E BB1 administration also produced an increase in occludin expression and localization at the apical membrane surface and junctional areas, as assess by immunostaining. (Occludin, green; nuclei, blue). Scale bars = 10 μm ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗∗P < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001, NS (not significant). (E). BB1 oral-gastric gavage administration produced a cytoplasmic-to-apical membrane translocation of TOLLIP in mouse intestinal epithelial cells (F). The BB1-induced apical membrane TOLLIP recruitment was inhibited in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice (TOLLIP, green; occludin, red; (Leica SP8 confocal microscope).

Next, the regulatory role of intestinal epithelial cell TLR-2 complex on the upregulation of the mouse intestinal barrier was determined. BB1 oral-gastric gavage resulted in an increase in intestinal tissue TLR-2 and TLR-6 expression without affecting TLR-1 in C57BL/6 wild-type (WT) mice (Fig. 7A). To determine the specific requirement of intestinal epithelial cell TLR-2, a conditional transgenic mouse having intestinal epithelial cell-specific knock-out of TLR-2, TLR-2 fl/fl Villin-cre mice (ΔIEC), was generated. The TLR-2 ΔIEC mice were generated by cross-breeding TLR-2 fl/fl mice with Villin-cre transgenic mice, utilizing the cre-lox system to inactivate the TLR-2 gene. As seen in Fig. 7B, the oral-gastric administration of BB1 (1 × 109 CFU/mL) in C57/BL6 WT mice resulted in an increase in apical membrane aggregation of TLR-2 (yellow-orange) in mouse intestinal epithelial cells (TLR-2, green; occludin, red; nucleus, blue). In contrast, TLR-2 was absent in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice, and BB1 had no effect on TLR-2 localization (Fig. 7B). The BB1-induced upregulation of mouse intestinal tissue occludin mRNA and protein was inhibited in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice (Fig. 7C–F). BB1 also did not produce cytoplasmic-to-apical membrane translocation of TOLLIP (Fig. 6F) in intestinal epithelial cells or a decrease in intestinal permeability (Fig. 7G) in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice. These data indicated that the intestinal epithelial TLR-2 was required for the BB1 upregulation of the occludin gene and intestinal TJ barrier in mice.Fig. 7Effect of BB1 on mouse intestinal tissue TLR-2 and occludin regulation.
A BB1 oral-gastric gavage produced an increase in small intestinal tissue expressions of TLR-2 and TLR-6 but not TLR-1. BB1 also produced an increase in apical membrane expression and localization of TLR-2 in mouse intestinal epithelial cells in the wild-type (WT) mice but not in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice, as assessed by immunostaining (TLR-2, green; occludin, red; nuclei, blue; magnification 63x) (B). The BB1-induced increase in mouse intestinal tissue occludin mRNA (C) and protein (D), densitometry (E) and apical membrane and junctional localization of occludin (occludin, green; nuclei, blue) (F), and decrease in intestinal permeability to dextran 10 kDa (G) were inhibited in the TLR-2 ΔIEC mice. ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001 versus control, NS (not significant).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Defective intestinal epithelial tight junction (TJ) barrier is a key pathogenic factor of intestinal inflammation in various inflammatory diseases of the gut, including IBD, celiac disease, and NEC, by allowing increased paracellular permeation of noxious luminal agents such as bacteria, bacterial antigens, digestive enzymes, and food by-products4,43,44. Preservation of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier prevents the development of intestinal inflammation in animal models of IBD and NEC and human IBD44–47. Commensal gut bacteria have an important modulatory role on gut microflora, intestinal homeostasis, and intestinal barrier function48,49. Bifidobacteria are the dominant gut commensals in infants and rapidly colonize the infant gut, and comprise up to 90% of the total bacterial community in early infancy21,50. Bifidobacterium bifidum, one of the earliest microbes to colonize the infant gut, is vital for maintaining a well-balanced and healthy gut environment21,51. The high abundance of Bifidobacteria declines with growth and development51. A disproportionate or pathological decrease in Bifidobacteria in the human gut is associated with various intestinal, metabolic, and immune diseases, including IBD, NEC, and obesity51,52. Bifidobacteria play a vital role in influencing the microbial environment with their genomes encoding a wide array of glycan-hydrolyzing enzymes that are crucial for species- and strain-specific carbohydrate-metabolizing capabilities26,53. While most probiotic strains studied to date have modest or no effect on intestinal TJ barrier27, a particular strain of Bifidobacterium bifidum, previously designated as BB1, produced a marked enhancement of intestinal TJ barrier function25. The BB1 oral-gastric administration also protected against the DSS-induced colitis in mice by preserving and maintaining the intestinal barrier and prevented the pro-inflammatory mediator (TNF-α or lipopolysaccharide) induced increase in intestinal permeability25,26. In contrast, Bifidobacterium bifidum strain, referred to as BB4, did not have a significant effect on intestinal TJ barrier function and did not protect against the DSS-induced colitis or inflammatory mediator-induced increase in intestinal permeability25,26. The molecular mechanisms and the microbe-host interactions responsible for the probiotic bacteria upregulation of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier remain unclear. The major purpose of this study was to delineate the mechanisms, including the host cell receptor complex and the target gene, responsible for the probiotic upregulation of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier, utilizing the two BB strains that have distinct effects on the TJ barrier.

The relative tightness or leakiness of the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier is, in part, dependent on the expression and composition of the transmembrane TJ proteins and is dynamically regulated by various signal-transduction pathways activated in response to the exogenous luminal factors54,55. The transmembrane TJ proteins, including the claudin family of proteins and occludin, extend transcellularly from the cytoplasm across the plasma membrane of the intestinal epithelial cells into the intercellular space to participate in the formation of the extracellular TJ barrier, which acts to selectively restrict the paracellular permeation of water-soluble molecules between the adjacent intestinal epithelial cells56. Claudin-2 is a key component of the TJ “pore” or small channel pathway and regulates the paracellular flux of ions, fluid, and small water-soluble molecules less than 4 Å in radius through the pore pathway57. Occludin is an essential component of the “leak” pathway (large-channel pathway) through which larger-sized macromolecules permeate paracellularly between the intestinal epithelial cells58. The TJ effector protein myosin light chain kinase (MLCK) also plays an important regulatory role in the leak pathway by stimulating the peri-junctional actin-myosin contraction and metabolic energy-dependent opening of the leak pathway54,59,60. In the studies described herein, we found that the BB1 upregulation of Caco-2 TJ barrier correlated with an increase in occludin protein but no other transmembrane TJ proteins, and that the siRNA-induced knock-down of occludin inhibited the BB1 upregulation of Caco-2 TJ barrier, indicating that the increase in occludin was required for the TJ barrier enhancement. Consistent with the previous studies showing occludin regulation of the leak pathway58, the BB1 increase in occludin resulted in a decrease in trans-epithelial flux of macromolecular probe inulin across the Caco-2 epithelial monolayers. As for the mechanism of BB1 increase in occludin, the occludin gene activity studies indicated that BB1 induces an increase in occludin promoter activity, and that inhibiting the occludin gene transcription or knocking down the occludin mRNA prevents the BB1 upregulation of occludin and the TJ barrier.

The plasma membrane-associated Toll-like receptors, TLR-1, TLR-2, TLR-4, TLR-5, and TLR-6, are present on the intestinal epithelial cell membrane surface and have a crucial role in recognizing microbe-associated molecular patterns (MAMP) to initiate the innate immune response61. In intestinal epithelial cells, TLR-2 are present on the apical membrane surface and TLR-4 and TLR-5 on the basolateral membrane surface21,25–27,36,37. Because of their apical membrane location, TLR-2 receptors are directly accessible to the MAMPS present in the intestinal lumen62. Bifidobacterium is a Gram-positive bacterium with a cell wall that possesses several unique features. The cell wall includes a thick peptidoglycan sacculus along with proteins, lipoteichoic acids, exopolysaccharides, and cell surface β-glucan/galactan21,63. Previous studies demonstrated that BB1 induces a focal aggregation of TLR-2 at the apical membrane surface of intestinal epithelial cells and attaches at these focal points of TLR-2 aggregation25. In the present study, the regulatory role of the specific TLR-2 heterodimer complex on occludin gene activity was investigated. TLR-2 is inactive in its monomeric state and requires dimerization with either TLR-1 or TLR-6 to form the biologically active heterodimer complex TLR-2/TLR-1 or TLR-2/TLR-664,65. Our BB1/TLR-2 receptor complex binding and activity studies in HEK-293 cells indicated that BB1 binds to the TLR-2/TLR-6, but not to the TLR-2/TLR-1, heterodimer complex on the cell membrane surface and induces the activation of the TLR-2 signal transduction pathway (Fig. 2A). The targeted knock-down of TLR-2 or TLR-6, but not TLR-1, in Caco-2 monolayers inhibited the BB1 upregulation of occludin gene and Caco-2 TJ barrier, confirming the regulatory role of the TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex. These results are consistent with the previous reports showing that TLR-2/TLR-6 complex binds to the diacyl lipopeptides, which are present on the Gram-positive bacterial cell wall, whereas the TLR-2/TLR-1 complex binds to the triacyl lipopeptides, which are typically present in the Gram-negative bacterial outer membrane35,66,67.

In immune cells, Toll-like receptor (TLR) activation (including TLR-2, TLR-4, and TLR-5) of pro-inflammatory response is mediated by the adaptor protein MyD88, which induces the activation of NF-κB and pro-inflammatory genes68,69. MyD88 serves as a master regulator of TLR-induced pro-inflammatory response by recruiting and phosphorylating IRAK-4 to form the myddosome multicomplex70,71, leading to the downstream activation of NF-κB and pro-inflammatory genes72,73. TOLLIP is an anti-inflammatory adaptor protein that is recruited to the myddosome complex and acts to suppress the hyperactive immune response39. In quiescent immune cells, TOLLIP is normally sequestered in the cytoplasm, bound to IRAK-139,41,71,74,75. In the setting of TLR-2 activation of MyD88 pro-inflammatory cascade in immune cells, TOLLIP-IRAK-1 complex is recruited to the activated myddosome and serves as a negative regulator of NF-κB to suppress the hyperimmune response39,69,76. Previous studies have shown that IRAK-1 and TOLLIP form the TOLLIP-IRAK-1 complex and have a close interdependent relationship77,78. These studies suggested that IRAK-1 modulates the TOLLIP activity and, conversely, TOLLIP affects the IRAK-1 activity22. TOLLIP is a multifunctional adaptor protein with domains that also mediate interactions beyond IRAK-1, including roles in endosomal trafficking and ubiquitin signaling. While our present study focused on its recruitment to the apical membrane through TLR-2/TLR-6 activation, in future studies, we also intend to investigate whether BB1-induced TOLLIP translocation influences other signaling pathways or trafficking events. Such studies could involve domain-specific mutagenesis and proteomic mapping of TOLLIP interaction partners under probiotic stimulation. In contrast to what has been previously described in the immune cells69, in the studies described herein in intestinal epithelial cells, we found that BB1 activation of TLR-2/TLR-6 receptor complex does not lead to MyD88 activation but instead produces a selective activation and recruitment of TOLLIP to the apical membrane surface. Our results suggested that TOLLIP plays a crucial regulatory role in the enhancement of the intestinal TJ barrier by regulating the occludin gene activation. The BB1 activation and apical membrane translocation of TOLLIP were dependent on TLR-2/TLR-6 complex phosphorylation of IRAK-1, and occludin gene upregulation was dependent on TOLLIP. Consistent with our results showing the requirement of TOLLIP and the lack of MyD88 involvement in the upregulation of occludin and TJ barrier in intestinal epithelial cells, previous studies also showed that BB1 does not activate NF-kB in intestinal epithelial cells and, in fact, inhibits the TNF-α-induced activation of NF-kB in Caco-2 monolayers25,26. Thus, BB1 activation of the TLR-2 receptor complex in intestinal epithelial cells appears to have an anti-inflammatory effect, mediated in part by TOLLIP activation, and leads to the suppression of NF-kB activation. In a separate publication, it was also reported that another probiotic bacterium, Lactobacillus acidophilus, referred to as LA1, produced an upregulation of the intestinal tight junction barrier in a TLR-2-dependent manner27. In our preliminary studies, we found that the LA1 increase in Caco-2 TJ barrier was dependent on TLR-2 complex activation of the occludin gene, suggesting that the TLR-2 signal transduction pathway upregulation of the occludin gene and the intestinal TJ barrier is not specific to BB1 but likely also applies to other TJ barrier-enhancing probiotic bacteria as well. As for the signaling pathway responsible for the activation of the occludin gene, we previously reported that BB1 induces the activation of PPAR-γ in a TLR-2-dependent manner26. We also recently found that the siRNA-induced knockdown of PPAR-γ inhibited the BB1 upregulation of occludin mRNA (data not shown), suggesting the involvement of the PPAR-g signaling pathway in the occludin gene regulation.

The intracellular mechanisms that mediate the BB1 upregulation of the intestinal tight junction barrier in the animal model system were also examined in live mice. In these studies, the effects of BB1 on the mouse intestinal barrier and the regulatory role of the TLR-2 complex were determined by recycling perfusion of isolated intestinal segments in live mice25,26. Similar to the results with the filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers, the BB1 upregulation of the mouse intestinal TJ barrier was preceded by the TLR-2 aggregation and TOLLIP recruitment to the apical membrane of intestinal epithelial cells and an increase in occludin protein. Our data indicated that BB1 oral-gastric gavage produces TOLLIP apical membrane recruitment and an increase in occludin expression in the intestinal epithelial cells of the C57/BL6 WT mice, and that intestinal epithelial cell-specifics knockdown of TLR-2 in C57/BL6 mice alone was sufficient to inhibit the BB1-induced upregulation of occludin and intestinal TJ barrier. Thus, these findings confirmed that the BB1 upregulation of the intestinal TJ barrier in live mice was also due to TLR-2 receptor complex-dependent recruitment of TOLLIP and increase in occludin expression in the intestinal epithelial cells. A limitation of our study is the use of a constitutive Villin-Cre system, which does not allow temporal control of TLR-2 deletion. Consequently, we cannot exclude potential developmental effects or compensatory changes arising from lifelong absence of epithelial TLR-2. Future studies using inducible Villin-CreERT2 models would help distinguish developmental versus adult-stage roles of epithelial TLR-2 and minimize potential systemic effects associated with lifelong deletion. Such approaches would also enable investigation of TLR-2 function during specific disease states or therapeutic interventions without confounding developmental influences.

In conclusion, our data showed that the probiotic (BB1) upregulation of intestinal epithelial TJ barrier in filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers and in live mice was mediated by host intestinal epithelial cell occludin gene activation and increase in occludin protein. Our studies demonstrated in a step-wise manner that BB1 binds to the TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex on the intestinal epithelial cell, leading to the phosphorylation of IRAK-1, apical membrane translocation of TOLLIP, and TLR-2 signal transduction pathway activation of the occludin gene. These results show, for the first time, that TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex on the intestinal epithelial cell, and the anti-inflammatory adaptor protein TOLLIP play a central regulatory role in the probiotic bacteria upregulation of the host intestinal epithelial TJ barrier by targeting the occludin gene. These studies provide novel mechanistic insight into how probiotic bacteria interacts with the intestinal epithelial cells to modulate TJ barrier and identify TLR-2 receptor complex and TOLLIP as potential therapeutic targets to up-regulate the intestinal epithelial TJ barrier and prevent/treat intestinal inflammation.

sec.title: Reagents
sec.p: Cell culture media (Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM)), trypsin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), glutamine, penicillin, streptomycin, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from GIBCO-BRL (Grand Island, NY). Transwell permeable filters (3460) were purchased from Corning (Corning, NY). TOLLIP(HPA038622), IRAK-1(SAB4504246), P-IRAK4(SAB3500304), CLDN2 (SAB4503544), occludin (SAB3500301), and anti-actin (sc-47778) antibodies were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Anti-IRAK-4 (AB307259), TOLLIP (ab187198) TLR-2(EPR2078Y), TLR-6(ab37072), CLDN1 (AB131259), CLDN2(ab53032), CLDN8(AB308005), CLDN5 (ab131259) antibodies were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA). Horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies for Western blot analysis were purchased from Invitrogen (San Francisco, CA).

sec.title: Caco-2 intestinal epithelial monolayer trans-epithelial resistance (TEER) and paracellular flux studies
sec.p: Caco-2 cells (passage 18) were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and maintained at 37 °C in a culture medium as previously described25,79. For growth on filters, Caco-2 cells were plated on transwell plates for 3-4 weeks until the transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) reached 400–500 Ω*cm2. The electrical resistance of the filter-grown Caco-2 intestinal monolayers was measured by using an epithelial voltohm-meter (World Precision Instruments, Sarasota, FL) as previously reported25,26,58. The Caco-2 monolayer paracellular permeability was determined by measuring mucosal-to-serosal flux of a paracellular marker inulin (molecular weight 5000 g/mol) (radioactive tracer)26,58,60. All flux studies were carried out at 37 °C. All of the experiments were repeated three to five times to ensure reproducibility.

sec.title: B. bifidum culture
sec.p: The B. bifidum ATCC 35914, ATCC 11863, ATCC 29521, ATCC BAA-2850, and ATTC 15696 were purchased from ATCC. ATCC 35914, originally isolated from the feces of a breast-fed infant, ATCC 35914 is a well-characterized probiotic strain noted for its strong adhesion to intestinal epithelial cells, high hydrophobicity, and robust auto-aggregation capacity80. These traits support enhanced persistence within the gut and potent barrier-modulating effects. ATCC BAA-2850, also derived from human fecal material, BAA-2850 exhibits minimal adhesive ability, lower hydrophobicity, and weaker aggregation characteristics compared to ATCC 35914. Consistent with previous studies25 and our findings, BAA-2850 does not significantly alter tight junction integrity or occludin expression. Both strains were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and cultured under identical conditions in MRS broth supplemented with 0.05% L-cysteine at 37 °C under anaerobic conditions. These strain-specific differences underscore the importance of mechanistic evaluation of individual probiotic strains, as recommended by FAO/WHO guidelines. These bacteria were grown overnight in MRS broth (Difco, Detroit, MI) at 37 °C. Live bacteria were spun-down by centrifuging at 12,000 × g for 10 min and, and the resulting pellets were stored at −80 °C for further experiments. Supernatants were rapidly prepared under anaerobic, sterile conditions, separated from pelleted bacteria, sterile-filtered, and subsequently diluted in DMEM (without FBS or antibiotics) for downstream experiments. For treating Caco-2 monolayers, the bacterial pellet was suspended in DMEM and diluted to OD600nm = 0.135 (equivalent to 1 × 108 colony-forming units (CFU)) in the same medium, and applied to the apical surface of cell monolayers, as previously described25.

sec.title: siRNA transfection
sec.p: Targeted SMARTpool siRNAs were obtained from Dharmacon (Lafayette, CO, USA). Caco-2 monolayers were transiently transfected using the DharmaFECT 4 transfection reagent (cat# T-2004-01). Caco-2 monolayers (5 × 105 cells per filter) were seeded into a 12-well transwell plate and grown to confluence. Caco-2 monolayers were then washed twice with PBS, and 0.5 mL of Accell Media obtained from Dharmacon (cat# B-00500-500; Lafayette, CO, USA) was added to the apical compartment of each filter, and 1.5 mL was added to the basolateral compartment of each transwell filter. Five nanograms of the siRNA and 2 µL of DharmaFect transfection reagent were added to the apical compartment of each filter. Non-target (NT) siRNA was used as a control for each experiment. The BB experiments were carried out 72 h after transfection, and the efficiency of silencing was confirmed by Western blot analysis of specific protein.

sec.title: BB binding and activation of TLR-2/TLR-6 or TLR-2/TLR-1 heterodimer complex
sec.p: BB1 binding to TLR-2 heterodimer complexes (TLR-2/TLR-6 or TLR-2/TLR-1) was assessed using the bacterial ligand–TLR-2 binding assay (InvivoGen, San Diego, CA). Genetically engineered HEK-293 reporter cell lines expressing membrane-bound TLR-2 in combination with either TLR-1 (TLR-6 knockout) or TLR-6 (TLR-1 knockout), each stably linked to a secreted embryonic alkaline phosphatase (SEAP) reporter under the control of an NF-κB promoter, were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 10⁴ cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO₂ atmosphere. BB1 was added to the 96-well plates containing the HEK-293 reporter cel lines. After an 18 h incubation period, culture supernatants were collected and SEAP activity was quantified using QUANTI-Blue™ reagent (InvivoGen) according to the manufacturer’s protocol34,81. Absorbance corresponding to SEAP enzymatic activity was measured at 650 nm (OD₆₅₀) using a SpectraMax i3x microplate reader (Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA). All experiments were performed in triplicate, and background-subtracted OD values were analyzed for relative NF-κB activation. Assays were repeated independently at least three times to confirm reproducibility.

sec.title: Immunofluorescence staining studies
sec.p: Caco-2 monolayers were fixed on permeable filters with ice-cold methanol for 20 min as previously described25,27. Mouse Intestinal tissues were collected at the indicated time points. Samples were sectioned at 10 μM longitudinal or transverse, sliced with a cryostat microtome (Microm Hm525 NX, Runcorn, UK) from the small intestine. Sections were blocked with 10% normal donkey serum for 60 min. Samples were incubated with anti-occludin antibody, anti-TOLLIP, or TLR-2 antibody (Cell Signaling Technology; Danvers, MA) overnight. The samples were then washed 3 times for 5 min in 0.1% PBS-Tween and blocked with Alexa-conjugated secondary antibody (Invitrogen). All the primary and secondary antibodies were used at the concentrations suggested by the manufacturers as previously described25,27,37. ProLong Gold antifade reagent (Cell Signaling), containing DAPI as a nuclear stain, was used to mount the samples on glass slides. Section images were acquired using the Leica SP8 confocal microscope. The images were processed using ZEN 3.2 (Blue edition) software, and fluorescence intensity was quantified using ImageJ software version 1.53k (NIH, 05-28-2024).

sec.title: Western blots
sec.p: Filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers were treated with BB1 as described in the experiments. At the end of the experiments, filter-grown Caco-2 monolayers were washed twice with cold HBSS and scraped, cell pellets were lysed with 100 lysis buffer (50 mM Tris·HCl, pH 7.5, 100 mM NaCl, 50 mM NaF, 5 mM EDTA, 40 mM b-glycerophosphate, 200 μM vanadate, 100 μM PMSF, 1 μg/ml leupeptin, 1 μg/ml pepstatin A, 40 mM paranitrophenyl phosphate, 1 μg/ml aprotinin, and 0.5% NP-40), and the cell lysates were placed in a microcentrifuge. Cell lysates were microcentrifuged for 15 min at 7,000 g to yield a clear lysate. Supernatant fluid was collected, and protein measurement was assessed with the Bio-Rad Protein Assay kit (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Hercules, CA) as previously described58,82. Laemmli gel loading buffer was added to the lysate containing 20 to 50 µg of protein and boiled for 7 minutes. After this, proteins were separated on SDS-PAGE gel. Proteins from the gel were transferred to the membrane (Trans-Blot Transfer Medium, Nitrocellulose Membrane; Bio-Rad Laboratories) overnight. The membrane was incubated for 2 h in blocking solution (5% dry milk in tris-buffered saline /Tween 20 buffer). The membrane was incubated with appropriate primary antibodies in blocking solution. After being washed in tris-buffered saline/1% Tween buffer, the membrane was incubated in appropriate secondary antibodies and developed with the Biorad ChemiDoc Imaging System (BioRad, Hercules, California). For all Western blot experiments, we used β-actin as the loading control.

sec.title: Quantification of gene expression using real-time PCR
sec.p: The real-time PCRs were performed using ABI Prism 7900 sequence detection system and TaqMan universal PCR master mix kit (Applied Biosystems, Branchburg, NJ) as previously described60,83. Each real-time PCR reaction contained 10 μL reverse transcription reaction mix, 25 μL 2× TaqMan universal PCR master mix, 0.2 μmol/L probe, and 0.6 μmol/L primers. Primer and probe design for the real-time PCR was made with Primer Express version 2 from Applied Biosystems. [The primers used in this study are as follows: occludin-specific primer pairs consisted of 5′-CCCCATCTGACTATGTGGAAAGA-3′ (forward), 5′-AAAACCGCTTGTCATTCACTTTG-3′ (reverse) probe specific for occludin consisted of FAM 5′-TGAAGATGCTGGCTCC-3′ TAMRA; the internal control glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH)-specific primer pairs consisted of 5′ CCACCCATGGCAAATTCC-3′ (forward), 5′-TGGGATTTCCATTGATGACCAG-3′ (reverse); probe specific for GAPDH consisted of JOE 5′-TGGCACCGTCAAGGCTGAGAACG-3′ TAMRA.] All runs were performed according to the default PCR protocol (50 °C for 2 min, 95 °C for 10 minutes, 40 cycles of 95 °C for 15 s, and 60 °C for 1 min). For each sample, real-time PCR reactions were performed in triplicate, and the average threshold cycle (Ct) was calculated. A standard curve was generated to convert the Ct to copy numbers. Expression of occludin mRNA was normalized with GAPDH mRNA expression. The average copy number of occludin mRNA expression in control samples was set to 1.0. The relative expression of occludin mRNA in treated samples was determined as a fold increase compared with control samples. For all PCR experiments we used GAPDH as the internal reference.

sec.title: Cloning and measurement of promoter activity
sec.p: Construction of the occludin promoter reporter plasmid was carried out using the pGL-3 basic luciferase reporter vector as previously described26,31,58. The primers used and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) conditions were as previously described31,58,83. Caco-2 cells (5 × 105 per filter) were seeded into a six-well transwell plate and grown to confluency. Caco-2 cells were transfected with the occludin promoter construct using Lipofectamine 2000, as previously described37,84. Luciferase activity was determined using the DUAL Luciferase assay kit (Promega, Madison, WI). The average activity value of the control samples was set to 1.0. The luciferase activity of the occludin promoter in treated samples was determined relative to the control samples.

sec.title: In vivo mouse intestinal permeability studies
sec.p: Studies were approved by the Penn State University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Institutional IACUC). Female and male C57BL/6 J wild-type mice, aged 9–12 weeks, were obtained from The Jackson Laboratory (Bar Harbor, ME). Female and male C57BL/6 TLR-2fl/fl Villin-cre/− and littermate TLR-2fl/fl Villin-cre −/− (control) mice were generated at Penn State University by mating TLR-2fl/fl mice (which were gifted by W Henry Bloom from Case Western Reserve University and the Cleveland Clinic) with Villin-cre/− mice (The Jackson Laboratory). C57BL/6, C57BL/6 TLR-2fl/fl Villin-cre and littermate TLR-2fl/fl Villin-cre −/− mice were pre-treated with B. bifidum via oral gastric gavage with 1 × 109 CFU of BB1 or BB4 strains. The effect of BB on intestinal permeability in an in vivo mouse model system was determined using a recycling intestinal perfusion method (as previously described).25,82 For in vivo studies, 1 × 109 CFU of BB in 200 μL of phosphate-buffered saline was administered daily by oral-gastric gavage, and mouse intestinal permeability was measured at different treatment periods. For intestinal permeability measurements, a 6–8 cm segment of mouse small intestine was isolated and cannulated with a small-diameter plastic tube (in an anesthetized mouse maintained at 1% isoflurane in oxygen) and continuously perfused with 5 mL of Krebs phosphate saline buffer for a 2 h perfusion period. An external recirculating pump was used to recirculate the perfusate at a constant flow rate (0.75 mL/minute). Intestinal permeability was assessed by measuring luminal-to-serosal flux rate of paracellular probe, Texas Red–labeled dextran (molecular weight, 10,000 g/mol), The water absorption was determined by using a nonabsorbable marker, sodium ferrocyanide (cat# 39660; Alfa Aesar, Ward Hill, MA) as described previously25–27,82.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p:
Statistical significance of differences between mean values was analyzed using one-way Anova for unpaired data and analysis of variance whenever required. All reported significance levels represent two-tailed P values. P < 0.05 was used to indicate statistical significance. All in-vitro experiments that used Caco-2 monolayers, including assessment of the TJ barrier function, biochemical and molecular studies, and kinase activity measurements, were performed in triplicates or quadruplicates (technical replicates) and were repeated at a minimum of three times for reproducibility (biological replicates). Data were analyzed using one-way ANOVA followed by an appropriate post hoc test (Dunnett’s or Tukey’s). Statistical significance was defined as P < 0.05. Western blot analyses and cell imaging studies were repeated three to four times, while animal studies were carried out individually, with each experimental group comprising three to six animals.Fig. 8Proposed schema of BB1-induced enhancement of intestinal epithelial tight junction (TJ) barrier.
BB1 attaches to the apical membrane surface of the host intestinal epithelial cells by binding to the TLR-2/TLR-6 heterodimer complex and induces the phosphorylation of IRAK-1 and apical translocation of TOLLIP, which, in turn, leads to the down-steam activation of occludin gene and occludin protein synthesis and occludin-dependent upregulation of the intestinal TJ barrier.

ack.p: This research project was supported by the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases R01-DK-121073-01 (to T. Y. Ma. and J. Lloyd Huck Chair in Medicine from Penn State College of Medicine (TYM)).

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: R.A. and T.Y.M. conceived and designed experiments and wrote the manuscript; R.A., R.A.-S., Y.G., and M.R. performed experiments; R.A., R.A.-S., and M.R. analyzed the data; R.A. and T.Y.M. interpreted results of experiments; R.A, and M.R. prepared figures; R.A. wrote the manuscript; T.Y.M. edited, revised the manuscript, project administration, and funding acquisition; and T.Y.M. approved final version of manuscript.

notes.title: Data Availability
notes.p: All raw data supporting the findings in Figs. 1-8 of this study are available within the paper.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
